title: Measurement of glucose in an insulin delivery catheter by minimizing the adverse effects of insulin preservatives
abstract: This disclosure teaches the concept, and method of creating, a dual use device intended for persons who take insulin. In one embodiment, the novel device is an insulin delivery cannula, the outer wall of which contains electrodes, chemical compounds and electrical interconnects that allow continuous glucose sensing and delivery of data to a remote device. Heretofore, the main problem in attempting to sense glucose at the site of insulin delivery has been the high current resulting from oxidation by the sensor of the preservatives in the insulin formulations. One means of eliminating these interferences is to poise the indicating electrode(s) of the sensor at a bias sufficiently low to avoid the signal from oxidation of the preservatives. One way of obtaining a glucose signal at a low bias is to use an osmium-ligand-polymer complex instead of conventional hydrogen peroxide sensing. Another is to use a size exclusion filter located in line with the insulin delivery tubing in order to remove the smaller phenolic preservative molecules while allowing the larger insulin molecules to pass unimpeded. These filtration concepts can also be more broadly applied, that is, the general concept of removal of unwanted drug formulation excipients from a drug delivery system.
claims:
Claims WE CLAIM: 1. A combined drug delivery cannula and continuous glucose sensor that measures glucose without interference from the drug excipient, said cannula being a hollow tube, the outer wall of which includes: an electrode layer with at least one indicating electrode, said layer underlying a redox-catalytic layer that includes an osmium compound bound to a ligand, and either glucose oxidase or glucose dehydrogenase. 2. The invention of claim 1 in which the drug is an insulin or insulin analog formulation. 3. The invention of claim 1 in which the indicating electrode material is gold, carbon, or platinum. 4. The invention of claim 1 in which the ligand is pyridine- or imidazole-based. 5. The invention of claim 4 in which the ligand is 4,4'-dimethyl- 2,2'-bipyridine. 6. The invention of claim 1 in which the osmium-ligand complex is bound to either poly (4 vinyl pyridine) or poly (1 -vinyl imidazole). 7. The invention of claim 1 in which the excipient is phenol and/or m-cresol. 8. The invention of claim 1 in which a reference electrode is also disposed on the outer cannula wall. 9. A combined insulin delivery cannula and continuous glucose sensor that measures glucose without interference from insulin preservatives, said cannula being a hollow tube, the outer wall of which contains the following layers: an insulating layer which underlies and is coupled to a metal layer, the thickness of which is at least 2 Î¼Î¹Î·, which underlies and is coupled to an electrode layer that includes at least one indicating electrode film, the thickness of which is less than 500 nm, said electrode layer underlying 

 a redox-catalytic layer that includes an osmium compound, bound to a pyridine- or imidazole-based ligand and to a pyridine- or imidazole-based polymer, and either glucose oxidase or glucose dehydrogenase. 10. The invention of claim 9 in which the insulin preservatives are phenol and/or m-cresol. 1 1 . The invention of claim 9 in which the insulating layer is polyimide or liquid crystal polymer. 12. The invention of claim 9 in which the metal layer is titanium, gold, or platinum. 13. The invention of claim 9 in which the indicating electrode film material is gold, carbon, or platinum. 14. The invention of claim 9 in which a reference electrode is also disposed on the cannula wall. 15. The invention of claim 9 in which the pyridine- or imidazole-based ligand is 4,4'-dimethyl- 2,2'-bipyridine. 16. The invention of claim 9 in which the pyridine- or imidazole-based polymer is poly(1 - vinylimidazole) or poly(4-vinylpyridine). 17. An infusion line, designed for the delivery of medication, containing a size exclusion filter designed for removal of an excipient in the medication formulation. 18. The invention of claim 17 in which the medication is an insulin or insulin analog formulation. 19. The invention of claim 17 in which the excipient is phenol and/or m-cresol. 20. The invention of claim 17 in which the size exclusion material is crosslinked dextran, carbon, alumina, silicate, combined alumina-silicate, Zeolite, a non-dextran carbohydrate, or a molecular sieve material. 21 . The invention of claim 18 in which at least 90% of the insulin or insulin analog is delivered to the user. 22. The invention of claim 19 in which at least 60% of the phenol and/or m-cresol is removed. 23. The invention of claim 17 in which the filtered eluent passes through a subcutaneous or intravenous delivery cannula which delivers said eluent to the body of a mammalian user. 24. The invention of claim 18 in which the filtered insulin-containing eluent passes through an insulin delivery cannula with glucose sensing capability before being delivered to the body of a mammalian user. 25. An insulin infusion line that contains an in-line size exclusion filter designed for removal of preservatives from an insulin formulation, said filter allowing the insulin or insulin analog to pass without removal. 26. The invention of claim 25 in which the preservative is phenol and/or m-cresol. 27. The invention of claim 25 in which the size exclusion material is crosslinked dextran, carbon, alumina, silicate, combined alumina-silicate, Zeolite, a non-dextran carbohydrate, or a molecular sieve material. 28. The invention of claim 25 in which at least 90% of the insulin or insulin analog is delivered to the user. 29. The invention of claim 26 in which at least 60% of the phenol and/or m-cresol is removed. 30. The invention of claim 25 in which the filtered insulin-containing fluid passes through an insulin delivery cannula which delivers the filtrate to the body of a mammalian user. 31 . The invention of claim 30 in which said insulin delivery cannula incorporates a glucose sensor disposed on its outer wall. 32. The invention of claim 30 in which a bias potential is maintained on conductive size exclusion filtration material immersed in conductive fluid so that the filtration efficiency is enhanced by electropolymerization of phenolic preservatives, 33. The invention of claim 32 in which said filter also containing a reference electrode located within the fluid path. 34. The invention of claim 32 in which the conductive filter material is carbon.
